retrieved_docs.title: Normal Labor: Physiology, Evaluation, and Management -- Treatment / Management -- Early versus delayed pushing
retrieved_docs.content: Notably, the largest and most recent multicenter trial included in this meta-analysis, published in 2018 and involving 2,404 of the 5,445 patients, found that early pushing was associated with lower rates of postpartum hemorrhage (2.3% versus 4.0%) and suspected neonatal sepsis (3.2% versus 4.4%) compared to delayed pushing. [38] Professional guidelines on this issue vary. The ACOG recommends early pushing, while the NICE advises informing parturients about the data on early versus delayed pushing and allowing them to make an informed decision. WHO and SOGC guidelines favor delaying pushing until the parturient feels the urge to push.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Differentiate the etiologies of postpartum hemorrhage based on patient history and physical exam. Identify the clinical features of postpartum hemorrhage, primarily focusing on blood loss. Apply resuscitative strategies to manage postpartum hemorrhage in pregnant individuals. Implement interprofessional team strategies to improve care coordination and communication, improving outcomes in patients with postpartum hemorrhage. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tranexamic Acid -- Indications -- Intravenous
retrieved_docs.content: Postpartum hemorrhage: The WOMAN Trial Collaborators in 2017 was a large multicenter, randomized, controlled, double-blinded study that showed that TXA reduces death in women with postpartum hemorrhage if given as soon as possible after bleeding onset. Meta-analysis further shows that TXA decreases the risk of life-threatening bleeding. [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Post-partum hemorrhage is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity in the United States; however, correct and timely institution of treatment can vastly improve patient outcomes. Patients who had PPH in a previous delivery are at risk of having PPH in subsequent deliveries. Implementation of standardized PPH protocols and interprofessional simulation training has helped improve patient outcomes. Additionally, increased efforts to enhance coordination among healthcare team members in response to PPH have contributed to reduced maternal morbidity. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for the causes of early PPH include: Uterine atony Lacerations Uterine inversion Retained placenta Uterine rupture Coagulopathy Endometritis Chorioamnionitis

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Care of the New Mother -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: During the first week of the postnatal period, severe hypertension, severe bleeding, and infection are the most common contributors to maternal deaths, while cardiovascular cause is the leading cause of late deaths. [27] Compared to developed countries such as Norway and New Zealand, the US has significantly lagged in providing adequate prenatal care. US mortality and morbidity are significantly higher ( 17.4 % vs. 1.7 % ), and the US has a significantly lower number of maternal healthcare providers, such as obstetricians and midwives ( 19 vs. 65 per 1000 live births ). Earlier postpartum visits are mandatory to evaluate for resolution of postpartum blues and other chronic medical conditions such as hypertension and diabetes and to improve both maternal and neonatal mortality and morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Postpartum Hemorrhage Management Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: In patients with continued PPH due to any etiology unresponsive to initial interventions, other management options include hypogastric artery ligation, uterine artery embolization, and hysterectomy. Uterine artery embolization may be considered in a patient who is stable with persistent bleeding. Fluoroscopy is used to identify and occlude bleeding vessels. While those who are unstable are not a candidate for this modality, benefits include uterine conservation and preservation of fertility. However, studies have demonstrated that patients who have undergone uterine artery embolization have an increased risk of infertility, preterm delivery, and intrauterine growth restriction.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: An interprofessional approach is mandatory for the required outcomes in the management of PPH, including: Obstetrics and gynecology Maternal-fetal medicine General surgery Anesthesiology Hematology Urology Emergency medicine Laboratory personnel Interventional radiology

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- General Postpartum Hemorrhage Management
retrieved_docs.content: The approach to treating PPH is focused on the patient's resuscitation when identifying and treating the underlying cause. Maintaining the patient's hemodynamic stability ensures continued perfusion to vital organs. As soon as PPH is identified, 2 large bore peripheral intravenous (IV) catheters (14- or 16G) should be placed, and maternal blood type and antibody screen should be confirmed in anticipation of aggressive corrective measures. Direct assessment of cumulative blood loss is essential, and a focus on early initiation of protocols for releasing blood products and massive transfusion protocols is necessary. Crystalloid and colloid IV fluids should be administered as indicated, and clinicians should also evaluate patients to identify the underlying cause and tailor treatments. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Cesarean Delivery -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The comparison between closing the hysterotomy in 1 versus 2 layers has been studied. Short-term outcomes, such as infectious morbidity, pain, blood transfusion, and hospital readmission, were not significantly different between the 2 techniques. [35] Data on whether a single-layer closure reduces operative time are mixed. [27] [29] However, for women desiring a future trial of labor, evidence suggests that a 2-layer closure improves residual myometrial thickness, enhances scar healing, and reduces the risk of uterine rupture in subsequent pregnancies. [27] [36] [37] An unlocked closure technique may also be preferable to a locked technique. [36] [37] A recent study suggested that endometrium-free closure of the uterus was associated with a decrease in placental abnormalities, such as placenta accreta, in subsequent pregnancies. [38] Ongoing research is exploring this specific aspect of uterine closure during cesarean section.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Intrapartum and Postpartum Care
retrieved_docs.content: Continuous cardiotocography is typically used to monitor patients and their fetuses during PTL. GBS prophylaxis and magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection should be continued through delivery. Postpartum, delayed cord clamping is advantageous for preterm neonates. Delayed cord clamping is associated with higher initial hematocrits, diastolic blood pressures, and circulating blood volume but with lower resuscitation rates, blood transfusion, and death before discharge. [59] [60] [61]  Results from a 2023 randomized clinical trial involving 204 neonates found that delayed cord clamping between 30 and 60 seconds is safe and effective. However, waiting 120 seconds was associated with higher rates of neonatal polycythemia and longer durations of phototherapy. [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Pulmonary Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Tranexamic Acid
retrieved_docs.content: With the success of the World Maternal Antifibrinolytic (WOMAN) and Clinical Randomisation of an Antifibrinolytic in Significant Hemorrhage 2 (CRASH-2) trials in postpartum hemorrhage and trauma, respectively, there have been some case reports of the use of intravenous tranexamic acid (TXA) or nebulized TXA in pulmonary hemorrhage with some success. [21] Results from a recent randomized controlled trial of 47 patients showed 96% resolution of hemoptysis in 5 days with inhaled TXA 500 mg 3 times a day versus the placebo group with 50%. Patients with hemoptysis greater than 200 mL in 24 hours or with hemodynamic or respiratory instability were excluded. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Vaginal Bleeding -- Evaluation -- Evaluation of Reproductive-Aged Patients
retrieved_docs.content: Workup in the Puerperium: Postpartum hemorrhage is also diagnosed clinically based on estimates of blood loss and physical exam findings (eg, uterine atony, obstetric lacerations). A CBC, coagulation panel, and type and cross-match are all indicated if bleeding does not rapidly resolve with uterotonic agents. If lochia is heavier than expected or prolonged, an ultrasound may be indicated to look for evidence of retained products of conception. A persistently elevated hCG level suggests GTD.

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Tocolysis
retrieved_docs.content: Oxytocin receptor antagonists:  These drugs are commonly used in Europe and are unavailable in the United States. Atosiban is the most frequently used drug in this class and is considered an acceptable first-line alternative to nifedipine in the WAPM-PMF guidelines. [2] A randomized clinical trial of 510 individuals comparing 48-hour courses of oral nifedipine to intravenous atosiban in patients with threatened PTL found no difference in the percentage of patients still pregnant after 48 hours and in composite perinatal outcomes. [52] Atosiban has relatively few maternal adverse effects beyond injection site reactions, and there are no absolute contraindications to its use beyond allergy to the medication.

retrieved_docs.title: Postpartum Hemorrhage -- Treatment / Management -- Additional Postpartum Hemorrhage Management Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy is typically indicated in the setting where less invasive measures for postpartum hemorrhage have failed or if the suspected reason for postpartum hemorrhage (eg, morbidly adherent placenta) is elucidated. A midline vertical abdominal incision should be considered to maximize exposure; however, the existing incision may be used if the patient had a Cesarean delivery. Vascular ligation sutures may be attempted to decrease pulse pressure in the uterus. Bilateral uterine artery ligation (O'Leary sutures) or bilateral utero-ovarian ligament ligation sutures may be placed. Hypogastric artery ligation can also be performed; however, as this procedure entails a retroperitoneal approach, the approach is rarely used. The definitive treatment for postpartum hemorrhage is a hysterectomy. A peripartum hysterectomy is associated not only with permanent sterility but also an increased surgical risk with a higher risk of bladder and ureteral injury. Supracervical hysterectomy may be performed alternately as a faster surgery with potentially fewer complicated risks. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 734
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad category of maternal complications that encompasses several specific conditions. It has as child entities postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة), failure to induce labor (فشل تحريض المخاض), and fetal death (موت الجنين). In addition, this category is classified as obstetric cervical tear (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic obstetric hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). Together, these interrelated complications illustrate the diverse and serious events that can arise during the postpartum period and influence the clinical management of the mother.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: tissue disorder categories, which in turn relate to general labor‑and‑delivery care indications. The umbrella of “Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother” integrates distinct clinical outcomes—postpartum hemorrhage, fetal death, cervical laceration, and pelvic hematoma—demonstrating the interconnectedness of maternal tissue pathology, obstetric injuries, and the need for comprehensive postpartum management. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in linking anatomical abnormalities to potential obstetric complications and in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies during labor, delivery, and the puerperium.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: maternal and fetal outcomes. It includes postpartum hemorrhage (نزف ما بعد الولادة) and fetal death (موت الجنين) as child conditions, and it is classified under specific obstetric injuries such as cervical laceration (تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم) and pelvic hematoma (ورم دموي توليدي حوضي). This reflects the heterogeneous nature of postpartum complications, which can range from severe bleeding to tissue injury and fetal loss. The same complications category is also classified as an “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable,” underscoring that many of these events are captured under broad, non‑specific labor‑and‑delivery care codes. The “Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable” entity has a broader relationship to both the pelvic abnormality group and to other obstetric conditions such as “Unspecified

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2388
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage during pregnancy, including the specific conditions abruptio placenta and placenta previa, are classified together as types of obstetric hemorrhage. This classification groups these distinct pregnancy‑related bleeding disorders, emphasizing their shared relevance to maternal health and guiding clinical assessment and management of hemorrhagic complications in pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: organizes general obstetric procedure categories (إجراء توليدي, Antepartum Procedures, Obstetrics incision) and specific clinical actions—ranging from diagnostic sampling and membrane management to fetal stimulation, reduction of multiple gestations, and surgical treatment of ectopic pregnancies—providing a comprehensive framework for coordinating obstetric care across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and postpartum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 773
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a broad obstetric category that encompasses specific child complications such as نزف ما بعد الولادة (post‑partum hemorrhage) and موت الجنين (fetal death). This category is also classified as تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (pelvic obstetric hematoma), indicating that cervical tears and pelvic hematomas represent particular subtypes within the spectrum of maternal management complications during the puerperium. Recognizing that نزف ما بعد الولادة, موت الجنين, تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم, and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي are interrelated components of Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is essential for comprehensive assessment and effective care of the mother in the post‑delivery period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1053
retrieved_docs.content: Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium is a broad diagnostic category that includes COMPLICATIONS MAINLY RELATED TO PREGNANCY. Within this category, a specific manifestation is Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is further classified as Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage. Recognizing that spotting in an unspecified trimester represents an early‑trimester hemorrhagic event and is considered a pregnancy‑related complication helps clinicians organize maternal conditions that affect pregnancy, delivery, or the puerperium and guides appropriate assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 761
retrieved_docs.content: a parent category for hemorrhage in pregnancy, and the higher‑level groups “pregnancy and puerperal disorders” and “maternal pregnancy complications” (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم) are also listed as parents of hemorrhage in pregnancy. This hierarchical structure underscores that hemorrhage in pregnancy is a specific disorder within the larger spectrum of gestational abnormalities and maternal obstetric disorders. Overall, the knowledge base connects hemorrhage in pregnancy with numerous specific bleeding scenarios, coagulation disorders, placental pathologies, and other pregnancy‑related complications, emphasizing the importance of recognizing these interrelated conditions for comprehensive maternal care and effective management during the antenatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2912
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy-Related Procedures is a broad category of obstetric interventions that encompasses specific techniques such as بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery, each classified as a child procedure within this group. Both بضع الفرج and Vaginal Assisted Delivery represent assisted vaginal delivery methods used to facilitate safe childbirth when spontaneous vaginal delivery is not optimal, highlighting the importance of tailored procedural options in maternal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 989
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a diagnostic category that includes specific obstetric injuries such as تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (obstetric pelvic hematoma). These maternal complications can result in severe outcomes, including موت الجنين (fetal death). The category is also associated with an Unspecified indication for care or intervention related to labor and delivery, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, indicating that these complications may occur in clinical situations where the precise indication for treatment is not clearly defined. In addition, Unspecified disproportion, unspecified as to episode of care or not applicable, is described as having a broader relationship with the same Unspecified indication for care or intervention, suggesting that fetal‑maternal size mismatch is part of the wider set of ambiguous labor‑delivery scenarios that can give rise to the aforementioned maternal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2656
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of birth; puerperium affecting management of mother is a clinical category that encompasses both تمزق توليدي في عنق الرحم (obstetric cervical tear) and ورم دموي توليدي حوضي (obstetric pelvic hematoma). This category is also associated with the adverse outcome موت الجنين (fetal death), indicating that cervical tears and pelvic hematomas occurring in the postpartum period can contribute to fetal mortality and require careful management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 943
retrieved_docs.content: hemorrhage and placental bleeding. Collectively, these interrelated entities and relationships illustrate how various uterine and hemorrhagic conditions are organized under the overarching concepts of pregnancy complications, maternal antenatal events, and broader obstetric disorder categories, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing these interconnected obstetric conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 719
retrieved_docs.content: and hemorrhage in pregnancy (نزف في الحمل), underscoring that these specific conditions are particular examples of pregnancy‑related complications. This network of relationships highlights the interconnection of hemorrhagic events with various maternal, placental, and coagulation disorders, emphasizing the importance of recognizing and managing hemorrhage in pregnancy within comprehensive obstetric care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 916
retrieved_docs.content: previa without hemorrhage). Although the latter denotes a previa without active bleeding, it is grouped together with hemorrhage in pregnancy to emphasize its close clinical relationship and potential progression to bleeding. Overall, the knowledge base links inverted uterus and various hemorrhagic conditions to the maternal antenatal period, situates hemorrhage in pregnancy within the spectrum of gestational abnormalities and puerperal disorders, and delineates multiple specific hemorrhagic subtypes—ranging from placental previa variants to coagulation‑related parturient hemorrhages—thereby providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing pregnancy‑related complications.
